response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"Look, I spend a good amount of time online, you know? Like, hours every day. And it’s getting really messy out there with all this fake stuff people post. It makes my head spin, honestly. It’s like nobody is telling the truth anymore, and that's exhausting. When you see something shocking or super dramatic—maybe about politics or even just some gossip from Milan—you gotta stop right away. Don't just react because it sounds juicy or makes you angry; take a breath first. Think about where it came from. Who posted it? Is it a real person, or is it some account that looks fake, maybe with too many exclamation points and no proper name? A lot of times, these things are put out by people who just want attention, so they get likes and comments. It feels like they don't even care if it messes up other people's lives. 

My approach, especially when I’m cooking or doing my yoga—when my mind is calm—is to treat everything online like a meal: you gotta check the ingredients first. Don't just eat what’s served on a silver platter without tasting it. If you see a big claim, maybe something that sounds too crazy to be true, don't share it immediately. Instead, take five minutes. Do a quick search on Google or another reliable site. See if other real news sources are talking about the same thing and saying the same thing. Also, remember that sometimes people post things just because they feel strong emotions—they’re angry or really excited. Those feelings can make them careless with the facts. It's like when I try a new recipe; if I rush it, the taste is all wrong. You gotta be patient and methodical.

I also think about who I’m spending time with online. If my friends are always sharing stuff without checking anything, it makes me feel uneasy. So, I try to focus on connecting with people who are smart, reliable, and who talk things through calmly. It's not just about blocking bad accounts; it’s about curating your feed so that you only see quality stuff. If something makes you instantly furious or deeply worried without any real reason, chances are, it’s fake, or at least exaggerated until it hurts the truth. Staying calm and being skeptical—that’s the best defense we have against all this noise.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I spend a good amount of time online, you know? Like, hours every day. And it’s getting really messy out there with all this fake stuff people post. It makes my head spin, honestly. It’s like nobody is telling the truth anymore, and that's exhausting. When you see something shocking or super dramatic—maybe about politics or even just some gossip from Milan—you gotta stop right away. Don't just react because it sounds juicy or makes you angry; take a breath first. Think about where it came from. Who posted it? Is it a real person, or is it some account that looks fake, maybe with too many exclamation points and no proper name? A lot of times, these things are put out by people who just want attention, so they get likes and comments. It feels like they don't even care if it messes up other people's lives. 

My approach, especially when I’m cooking or doing my yoga—when my mind is calm—is to treat everything online like a meal: you gotta check the ingredients first. Don't just eat what’s served on a silver platter without tasting it. If you see a big claim, maybe something that sounds too crazy to be true, don't share it immediately. Instead, take five minutes. Do a quick search on Google or another reliable site. See if other real news sources are talking about the same thing and saying the same thing. Also, remember that sometimes people post things just because they feel strong emotions—they’re angry or really excited. Those feelings can make them careless with the facts. It's like when I try a new recipe; if I rush it, the taste is all wrong. You gotta be patient and methodical.

I also think about who I’m spending time with online. If my friends are always sharing stuff without checking anything, it makes me feel uneasy. So, I try to focus on connecting with people who are smart, reliable, and who talk things through calmly. It's not just about blocking bad accounts; it’s about curating your feed so that you only see quality stuff. If something makes you instantly furious or deeply worried without any real reason, chances are, it’s fake, or at least exaggerated until it hurts the truth. Staying calm and being skeptical—that’s the best defense we have against all this noise.
